mixed.question: Which country's capital city is located at a higher altitude than the summit of Australia's highest peak, Mount Kosciuszko?
mixed.fact: Mount Kosciuszko is only 2,228m tall — La Paz, Bogotá, and Quito all sit higher!

mixed.question: True or False: The African continent contains a glacier that is visible from the equator.
mixed.fact: Mount Kenya, almost exactly on the equator, still has shrinking glaciers visible from its slopes.

mixed.question: Which European country has more than 100,000 lakes but only borders one other country?
mixed.fact: Portugal has over 100,000 small lakes and reservoirs and only borders Spain — often overlooked!

mixed.question: In early 2026, satellite data confirmed that which island had measurably grown by over 800 acres due to ongoing volcanic activity since 2023?
mixed.fact: Nishinoshima has been erupting intermittently since 2013 and keeps dramatically expanding its landmass.

mixed.question: In 2025, what surprising capability was demonstrated in tardigrades by Japanese researchers at the University of Tokyo?
options: Their proteins can shield human DNA from radiation
They can reproduce in deep space
mixed.fact: Tardigrade-derived proteins were shown to reduce radiation damage to human cultured cells by over 40%.

mixed.question: Which element was first predicted by AI-driven materials science in 2025 to form a stable compound with neon under extreme pressure?
mixed.fact: Sodium-neon compounds were predicted stable above 160 GPa, challenging neon's reputation as totally inert.

mixed.question: What did NASA's DAVINCI probe data, analyzed in late 2025, suggest about Venus's atmosphere?
options: Its clouds may harbor phosphine-producing chemistry
It rotates faster than the planet
It has a stable ozone layer
mixed.fact: Venus's atmosphere 'super-rotates' at 60 times the speed of the planet's surface — still poorly understood.

mixed.question: How many times heavier is a teaspoon of neutron star material compared to Mount Everest?
mixed.fact: Both weigh roughly 6 billion tons — a teaspoon of neutron star matches an entire mountain!

mixed.question: Which animal can survive being completely frozen solid for months and then thaw back to life?
mixed.fact: These caterpillars freeze solid each winter for up to 14 years before finally becoming moths.

mixed.question: True or False: There is a species of shrimp whose claw snap is so powerful it generates temperatures hotter than the sun's surface.
mixed.fact: The pistol shrimp's cavitation bubble reaches 4,700°C — nearly matching the sun's 5,500°C surface!

science.question: Which chemical element was named after a goblin from Germanic folklore due to its troublesome properties for miners?
science.fact: Cobalt comes from 'Kobold,' a mischievous goblin, because its ores poisoned miners and ruined copper smelting.

science.question: In 2025, scientists synthesized element 120 (unbinilium) for the first time. Which research facility announced this breakthrough?
science.fact: RIKEN used intense calcium-48 beams fired at californium targets in years-long experiments to detect just a few atoms.

science.question: True or False: Gallium metal will melt if you hold it in your hand.
science.fact: Gallium's melting point is just 29.76°C (85.6°F), comfortably below human body temperature.

science.question: Which noble gas can form stable compounds with fluorine, contradicting the long-held belief that noble gases are completely inert?
science.fact: Neil Bartlett shocked chemistry in 1962 by creating xenon hexafluoroplatinate, the first noble gas compound.

science.question: What is the name of the persistent weather phenomenon where the stratosphere above the Arctic suddenly warms by up to 50°C in days?
science.fact: SSW events can reverse stratospheric winds and trigger extreme cold snaps thousands of miles away at ground level.

science.question: Which surprising climate milestone was crossed in late 2025, according to the Copernicus Climate Change Service?
options: First full calendar year averaging 1.5°C above pre-industrial levels
Arctic had its first ice-free September
science.fact: 2024–2025 data confirmed the first full year breaching the 1.5°C Paris Agreement threshold as a running average.

science.question: How many distinct categories exist on the Saffir-Simpson Hurricane Wind Scale?
science.fact: Category 5 starts at 157 mph, but some scientists have proposed adding Category 6 for storms exceeding 192 mph.

science.question: Which disease is caused by prions — misfolded proteins that contain no DNA or RNA yet are still infectious?
science.fact: Prions can force normal brain proteins to misfold, creating chain reactions that riddle the brain with sponge-like holes.

science.question: What counterintuitive medical condition causes people to feel pain from stimuli that are normally painless, such as a light touch?
science.fact: In allodynia, even a gentle breeze on skin can trigger excruciating pain due to rewired nerve signaling.

science.question: Which parasitic disease, often called a 'neglected tropical disease,' is estimated to still affect over 200 million people annually worldwide?
science.fact: Schistosomiasis is spread by freshwater snails; the parasitic worms can live inside human blood vessels for decades.

history.question: Which Viking explorer is believed to have reached North America nearly 500 years before Columbus, establishing a settlement at L'Anse aux Meadows?
history.fact: L'Anse aux Meadows in Newfoundland is the only confirmed Viking site in North America, dating to around 1000 AD.

history.question: In 2025, archaeologists in Sweden unearthed a Viking-era burial containing a surprising item from which distant civilization?
history.fact: Vikings traded extensively with Byzantium — some even served as the emperor's elite Varangian Guard bodyguards.

history.question: True or False: Viking warriors commonly wore horned helmets in battle.
options: True — but only chieftains wore them
False — they wore pointed helmets instead
history.fact: The horned helmet myth was popularized by a costume designer for an 1876 Wagner opera production.

history.question: What was a 'holmgang' in Viking society?
options: A formal duel to settle disputes
A rite of passage for young warriors
history.fact: Holmgang literally means 'island walk' because duels were often fought on small islands to prevent escape.

history.question: Which surprising act did the Continental Congress authorize privateers to perform during the American Revolution?
history.fact: Over 1,700 letters of marque were issued — American privateers captured roughly 3,000 British vessels during the war.

history.question: Who was the highest-ranking American officer to defect to the British during the Revolutionary War?
history.fact: Arnold was promised £20,000 for surrendering West Point, but the plot's failure meant he received only £6,315.

history.question: How many of the original thirteen colonies voted in favor of the resolution for independence on July 2, 1776?
history.fact: New York abstained from the July 2 vote because its delegates lacked authorization — they approved it days later.

history.question: Which ancient Mesopotamian city is considered the world's first true metropolis, reaching an estimated population of 80,000 around 2900 BCE?
history.fact: Uruk's population made up roughly 1% of the entire world's people at the time — an astonishing concentration.

history.question: What surprising mathematical concept did ancient Babylonians use on clay tablets over 1,000 years before Pythagoras?
options: The value of pi to two decimals
history.fact: Tablet Plimpton 322, dated to ~1800 BCE, contains a sophisticated list of Pythagorean triples predating Greek mathematics.

history.question: In ancient Mesopotamia, what unusual substance was used as a common form of payment for workers alongside barley?
history.fact: Sumerian workers received daily beer rations — one of the oldest known labor compensation systems in human history.

popculture.question: Which superhero film's villain was originally written as a woman before being changed to male just weeks before filming began?
popculture.fact: Marvel changed the Mandarin from a woman named 'Maya Hansen' as the main villain due to toy sales concerns.

popculture.question: In Marvel Comics, how many issues did the original Groot speak full sentences before becoming the 'I am Groot' character?
options: Zero — he always said only 'I am Groot'
popculture.fact: Groot debuted in 1960's Tales to Astonish #13 as an eloquent, villainous talking tree invading Earth!

popculture.question: Which 2025 superhero film became the first to use real-time LED volume technology for more than 90% of its runtime?
popculture.fact: The Fantastic Four: First Steps used a massive LED stage to recreate 1960s New York in stunning detail.

popculture.question: True or False: Batman co-creator Bill Finger received no official credit on Batman comics or media until 2015 — 76 years after Batman's debut.
popculture.fact: DC only added 'Batman created by Bob Kane with Bill Finger' after a 2015 agreement with Finger's estate.

popculture.question: Which streaming series revealed in its finale that its entire narrative took place inside a single day, surprising most viewers?
popculture.fact: The Bear Season 2's 'Fishes' episode and Season 2 finale both unfold in real-time single-day formats.

popculture.question: What was the most-watched non-English language series on Netflix as of early 2026?
popculture.fact: Squid Game Season 1 amassed over 1.65 billion viewing hours, crushing all other non-English Netflix records.

popculture.question: Which streaming platform introduced a controversial 'AI recap' feature in late 2025 that summarizes episodes you skipped?
popculture.fact: Amazon's AI recap feature sparked debate about whether it encourages viewers to skip content entirely.

popculture.question: The 'Distracted Boyfriend' stock photo meme originated from a series by which photographer?
popculture.fact: Antonio Guillem shot an entire jealousy-themed series — the famous image is just one of dozens in the set.

popculture.question: Which 2025 viral moment involved a live news broadcast where an animal unexpectedly stole a reporter's microphone, gaining over 200 million views?
popculture.fact: The Bali macaque grabbed the mic and appeared to 'interview' other monkeys, spawning countless remixes worldwide.

popculture.question: The 'Is This a Pigeon?' meme comes from which anime, where a character genuinely mistakes a butterfly for a pigeon?
options: The Brave Fighter of Sun Fighbird
popculture.fact: The android character Katori genuinely cannot distinguish insects from birds, making the scene unintentionally hilarious.

geography.question: Which country has a higher population density: Bangladesh or South Korea?
geography.fact: Bangladesh packs over 1,300 people per km², making it denser than almost every non-city-state nation on Earth.

geography.question: As of 2025, which African city surpassed Cairo to become the continent's most populous urban agglomeration?
geography.fact: Kinshasa's explosive growth made it Africa's largest city, with over 20 million residents by recent estimates.

geography.question: True or False: More than half of the world's projected population growth between 2025 and 2050 will occur in just eight countries.
options: It will be spread across at least 30 countries equally
Growth has already halted in most regions
geography.fact: The UN projects India, Nigeria, Pakistan, DR Congo, Ethiopia, Tanzania, Indonesia, and Egypt will drive most growth.

geography.question: Which country has one of the world's fastest-declining populations, losing roughly 1% of its people per year since 2020?
geography.fact: Bulgaria's population has shrunk from 9 million in 1989 to under 6.5 million, one of history's steepest peacetime declines.

geography.question: The Danakil Depression in the Afar Triangle, one of Earth's hottest places, sits at the junction of which three countries?
geography.fact: The Danakil Depression averages 34.4°C year-round and contains lava lakes and neon-colored hydrothermal fields.

geography.question: Which African nation contains the Richat Structure, a 40-km-wide geological formation sometimes called the 'Eye of the Sahara'?
geography.fact: Visible from space, the Richat Structure was once thought to be a meteor crater but is actually an eroded geological dome.

geography.question: Lake Malawi is shared by Malawi, Mozambique, and which third country that calls it 'Lake Nyasa'?
geography.fact: Lake Malawi holds more fish species than any other lake on Earth — over 1,000, most found nowhere else.

geography.question: Which desert receives so little rainfall that parts of it have had no recorded precipitation in over 500 years?
geography.fact: NASA uses the Atacama to test Mars rovers because its soil chemistry closely resembles the Martian surface.

geography.question: The Lut Desert (Dasht-e Lut) in Iran holds the record for the highest land surface temperature ever recorded by satellite. What was it?
geography.fact: The Lut Desert's ground surface hit 70.7°C in 2005 — hot enough to cook an egg on the sand.

geography.question: How many people live in Antarctica's largest 'city,' McMurdo Station, at its summer peak?
geography.fact: McMurdo has its own ATM, a bowling alley, and even a disc golf course despite being on volcanic rock.

sports.question: Which golfer holds the record for the largest margin of victory in any major championship, winning by 15 strokes?
sports.fact: Tiger Woods won the 2000 U.S. Open at Pebble Beach by 15 strokes — the most dominant major win ever.

sports.question: True or False: No left-handed golfer won a Masters Tournament until the 21st century.
sports.fact: Mike Weir became the first lefty Masters champion in 2003, followed by Phil Mickelson in 2004.

sports.question: At which golf major did Scottie Scheffler capture his second green jacket, reinforcing his dominance in 2025/2026?
sports.fact: Scheffler's consistency at Augusta has made him one of the most dominant Masters performers in recent years.

sports.question: Who is the only golfer to have won all four modern major championships on their first attempt at each?
sports.fact: No golfer has ever won all four majors on their debut appearance — even Bobby Jones needed multiple tries.

sports.question: How many career Grand Slam singles titles did Novak Djokovic accumulate by the end of 2025?
sports.fact: Djokovic's 24 Grand Slam titles tied Margaret Court's all-time record across both men's and women's tennis.

sports.question: Which Grand Slam tournament uniquely switched from grass courts to clay in 1978 before reverting back years later?
sports.fact: No Grand Slam switched grass to clay. The Australian Open moved from grass to hardcourt, and the US Open from clay to hardcourt.

sports.question: Who was the last unseeded player to win a men's singles Grand Slam title?
sports.fact: Ivanisevic entered Wimbledon 2001 as a wildcard ranked 125th and won — the only wildcard to win a Slam.

sports.question: Which NBA player holds the record for the most points scored in a single game?
sports.fact: Wilt's 100-point game in 1962 had no TV broadcast — only an audio recording of the final moments survives.

sports.question: Which NBA team won the fewest games in a single season (post-merger), setting the all-time futility record?
sports.fact: The Bobcats' 7-59 record in the lockout-shortened 2012 season gave them a .106 winning percentage — the worst ever.

sports.question: Who became the youngest player in NBA history to reach 10,000 career points?
sports.fact: LeBron reached 10,000 points at just 23 years and 59 days old — nearly a full year younger than Kobe Bryant.

food.question: In Sardinia, what illegal cheese is intentionally infested with live insect larvae and considered a delicacy?
food.fact: Casu marzu contains thousands of live maggots that can jump up to 15 centimeters when disturbed.

food.question: Which Southeast Asian fruit is so pungent it's banned on public transport in several countries, yet prized as the 'King of Fruits'?
food.fact: Durian's smell has been compared to rotting flesh, yet its custard-like flesh sells for premium prices worldwide.

food.question: In 2025, which insect-based snack was approved by the EU for widespread sale in supermarkets as a third novel food species?
food.fact: The EU now has three approved insect species for human consumption, reflecting a growing acceptance of entomophagy.

food.question: What Japanese delicacy must be prepared by a licensed chef because it contains tetrodotoxin, a poison 1,200 times deadlier than cyanide?
food.fact: Japanese fugu chefs train for at least three years before earning their license to serve this potentially lethal fish.

food.question: Which classic Italian pasta sauce from Rome is made with only guanciale, eggs, Pecorino Romano, and black pepper — no cream?
food.fact: Adding cream to carbonara is considered a culinary crime in Rome — the silky texture comes entirely from eggs and cheese.

food.question: True or False: The tomato was feared as poisonous in Italy for nearly 200 years after its arrival from the Americas before becoming a culinary staple.
options: It was only feared for 50 years
food.fact: Wealthy Europeans ate from lead-pewter plates; tomato acid leached lead out, actually causing poisoning and reinforcing the fear.

food.question: What is 'nduja, the fiery Italian specialty from Calabria?
food.fact: Nduja is so soft it's spread like butter, and its recipe likely traces back to a French charcuterie technique called andouille.

food.question: Which country consumes the most chocolate per capita, averaging over 8 kilograms per person annually?
food.fact: Despite being famous for chocolate, the Swiss eat nearly twice as much per person as Americans do each year.

food.question: What high-end chocolate variety, once dismissed as defective, is made from a naturally white-fleshed mutation of the cacao bean?
food.fact: Ruby chocolate was developed by Barry Callebaut in 2017 from ruby cacao beans — no berries or colorings are added.

food.question: How many cacao beans does it take, on average, to produce one pound (450g) of chocolate?
food.fact: A single cacao tree produces only enough beans annually to make roughly 2 pounds of finished chocolate.

music.question: Which K-pop group's member, Hueningkai, is notable for being half-Korean and half-Brazilian, a rarity in the industry?
music.fact: Hueningkai's father is American-Brazilian and his mother is Korean, making him uniquely multinational in K-pop.

music.question: In 2025, which Japanese city-pop classic from 1979 surpassed 1 billion YouTube streams, decades after its original release?
options: Stay With Me by Miki Matsubara
Ride on Time by Tatsuro Yamashita
music.fact: Plastic Love went viral in 2017 via a YouTube algorithm quirk and became the global face of city-pop.

music.question: True or False: BLACKPINK's Rosé was actually born in New Zealand, not Australia as commonly believed.
options: She was born in South Korea
music.fact: Rosé was born in Auckland, New Zealand, then moved to Melbourne, Australia, leading to the common misconception.

music.question: Which K-pop soloist released the 2025 album 'HOPE ON THE STREET VOL.2' blending street dance culture with hip-hop?
music.fact: J-Hope's street dance documentary series inspired his unique album concept merging movement with music.

music.question: Which opera contains an aria where the soprano must hit an F6, often called the highest note in standard operatic repertoire?
music.fact: The Queen of the Night's aria 'Der Hölle Rache' demands an F6, terrifying sopranos for over 230 years.

music.question: Who composed the 'Grosse Fuge,' a piece so radical that his publisher demanded he replace it with a different finale?
music.fact: Beethoven's Grosse Fuge was called 'incomprehensible' in 1826 but is now hailed as visionary proto-modernism.

music.question: How many operas did Rossini compose after the age of 37, despite living another 39 years?
music.fact: Rossini retired from opera at 37 after 'William Tell' and spent decades composing only small pieces and cooking.

music.question: Which Beatles song was recorded in a single marathon session of just 10 hours, including all orchestral overdubs?
music.fact: The famous orchestral crescendo in 'A Day in the Life' was recorded with 40 musicians told to improvise upward.

music.question: Who played the distinctive piccolo trumpet solo on The Beatles' 'Penny Lane'?
music.fact: David Mason was paid just £27 and 10 shillings for the iconic solo that defined the song's character.

music.question: What was unusual about The Beatles' 1968 single 'Lady Madonna' in terms of its credited instrumentation?
options: It featured no guitars at all
The saxophone was played by session musicians, not credited on the label
Ringo played piano instead of drums
It was the first Beatles single mixed in stereo only
music.fact: Four jazz saxophonists including Ronnie Scott played on the track but went entirely uncredited on release.

technology.question: Which 1993 video game famously had its entire soundtrack composed by a former member of the band Skinny Puppy?
technology.fact: Bobby Prince drew heavy inspiration from metal bands, but Doom's sound design also involved industrial music influences.

technology.question: What was the original working title of the Nintendo 64, used publicly at its 1995 reveal?
technology.fact: The 'Ultra 64' branding appeared on arcade cabinets for Cruis'n USA before the console even launched.

technology.question: In 2025, which classic game franchise saw its source code leaked, revealing a hidden developer room never accessed by players in 25+ years?
technology.fact: The leaked GoldenEye 007 source revealed a fully modeled but inaccessible developer testing room with joke textures.

technology.question: True or False: The Atari 2600 version of Pac-Man sold more copies than there were Atari 2600 consoles at the time of its release.
options: True — Atari overproduced expecting new console sales
False — it sold roughly half the install base
False — it was a commercial failure from day one
True — but only counting bundled copies
technology.fact: Atari manufactured 12 million Pac-Man cartridges for only 10 million console owners, contributing to the 1983 crash.

technology.question: Which company developed the first commercially available industrial robot, the Unimate, installed at a General Motors plant in 1961?
technology.fact: Unimate's first job was extracting hot die-cast metal parts — a task considered too dangerous for human workers.

technology.question: How many axes of movement does a standard industrial articulated robot arm typically have?
technology.fact: Six axes mimic the degrees of freedom of a human arm, enabling robots to reach any position and orientation.

technology.question: What counterintuitive problem in robotics describes why controlling a robot's end-effector position from joint angles is computationally trivial, but the reverse is extremely complex?
technology.fact: Inverse kinematics can have infinite solutions or none at all, making it far harder than forward kinematics.

technology.question: Which programming language, created in 1970, was originally designed to teach structured programming and was named after a 17th-century mathematician?
technology.fact: Niklaus Wirth named Pascal after Blaise Pascal, who built one of the earliest mechanical calculators at age 19.

technology.question: The esoteric programming language 'Malbolge' was designed to be as difficult as possible. How long after its creation was the first working program written?
technology.fact: The first Malbolge program wasn't written by a human — it was generated by a beam search algorithm in 1998.

technology.question: Which programming language introduced the concept of 'borrow checking' to enforce memory safety at compile time without a garbage collector?
technology.fact: Rust's borrow checker was so novel that it initially confused even experienced systems programmers transitioning from C++.

nature.question: Which frog species is known for incubating its eggs in its vocal sac, with fully formed froglets emerging from the father's mouth?
nature.fact: Male Darwin's frogs swallow fertilized eggs and brood them in their vocal sacs for over 50 days!

nature.question: True or False: Some species of sea snakes can absorb up to 33% of their oxygen through their skin while diving.
options: Only freshwater snakes can do this
Only juvenile sea snakes can do this
nature.fact: The blue-banded sea snake can breathe significantly through cutaneous respiration, supplementing its lungs underwater.

nature.question: In 2025, researchers discovered that the New Caledonian giant gecko, once thought extinct, was found surviving in which unexpected habitat?
nature.fact: Populations were found thriving in nickel-mining scrubland that scientists had dismissed as too degraded for large geckos.

nature.question: The tuatara of New Zealand has a 'third eye' on top of its head. What is its primary function?
nature.fact: The parietal eye has a lens and retina but is covered by scales in adults—it helps regulate hormonal light cycles.

nature.question: Which bird of prey can dive at speeds exceeding 240 mph, making it the fastest animal on Earth?
nature.fact: Peregrine falcons have special bony baffles in their nostrils to manage airflow during extreme high-speed dives.

nature.question: The Philippine eagle primarily hunts which surprising prey item that makes up a large part of its diet?
nature.fact: Despite being called the 'monkey-eating eagle,' colugos—not monkeys—are its most common prey in many forest habitats.

nature.question: How many cervical vertebrae does an owl have, allowing it to rotate its head up to 270 degrees?
nature.fact: Owls have 14 cervical vertebrae—double the human count—plus special blood-pooling systems to prevent strokes during rotation.

nature.question: Which dog breed has a unique 'split nose' (double nose) and was traditionally used for hunting jaguars in Bolivia?
nature.fact: The Double-Nosed Andean Tiger Hound is one of the rarest breeds, and locals believed the split nose enhanced scent tracking.

nature.question: True or False: Domestic cats have more bones than domestic dogs.
options: It depends on the breed of dog
nature.fact: Cats have around 230 bones while dogs have approximately 319—wait, actually cats have ~230 vs dogs ~321. Dogs have more!

nature.question: Which domesticated animal was shown in a 2025 study to recognize and respond differently to photographs of their owner's face versus strangers?
nature.fact: Pigs distinguished their caretaker's photo from strangers and showed measurable positive emotional responses to familiar faces.

film.question: Which South Korean film became the first non-English language movie to win the Academy Award for Best Picture?
film.fact: Parasite also won Best Director, Best Original Screenplay, and Best International Feature Film that same night.

film.question: In the 2025 Cannes Film Festival, which country's film won the Palme d'Or for only the second time in the nation's history?
film.fact: Japan's previous Palme d'Or was Shoplifters by Hirokazu Kore-eda in 2018.

film.question: Which Iranian director was sentenced to prison and banned from filmmaking yet secretly directed 'Taxi' entirely inside a cab?
film.fact: Panahi hid cameras on his dashboard and won the Golden Bear at Berlin despite his filmmaking ban.

film.question: The 1975 Indian film 'Sholay' was originally a box-office disappointment in its first week before becoming a massive hit. In which language was it made?
film.fact: Sholay ran for over five consecutive years at Mumbai's Minerva Theatre, an unbroken Indian cinema record.

film.question: Which director's debut feature was 'Pi' (1998), a paranoid thriller about a mathematician shot on high-contrast black-and-white 16mm film?
film.fact: Pi was made for just $60,000, partly funded by $100 donations from friends and family.

film.question: True or False: The Coen Brothers' 'Blood Simple' (1984) was partly financed by showing a fake trailer to potential investors in Minnesota.
options: It was financed entirely by a studio
film.fact: The Coens raised $1.5 million by screening a two-minute trailer they shot specifically to attract investors.

film.question: Which 2024 A24 arthouse film directed by Brady Corbet portrayed a Hungarian architect's experience in postwar America?
film.fact: The Brutalist runs over three and a half hours and includes an intermission, rare for modern releases.

film.question: Which silent film star performed all his own stunts, including clinging to the hands of a real clock face high above a street in 'Safety Last!' (1923)?
film.fact: Harold Lloyd was missing a thumb and forefinger from a 1919 accident yet still did the iconic stunt.

film.question: What was unusual about the 1927 film 'Sunrise: A Song of Two Humans' at the first Academy Awards?
options: It won Best Picture but was a silent film
It shared the top prize under a now-discontinued category
It was the first color film nominated
film.fact: Sunrise won 'Best Unique and Artistic Production,' a top-tier award category used only once in Oscar history.

film.question: How many feature films did legendary Golden Era director F.W. Murnau complete before his death at age 42 in 1931?
film.fact: Murnau died in a car crash one week before the premiere of his final film, Tabu.

politics.question: Which 2024 treaty became the first legally binding international agreement specifically targeting plastic pollution across the full lifecycle?
politics.fact: Negotiations nearly collapsed at 3 AM before a last-minute compromise on production caps saved the deal.

politics.question: True or False: The Treaty on the Prohibition of Nuclear Weapons (TPNW) has been signed by all five permanent UN Security Council members.
politics.fact: None of the nine nuclear-armed states have signed the TPNW, making it uniquely opposed by those it targets.

politics.question: The Svalbard Treaty of 1920 grants citizens of all signatory nations equal rights to do what on the archipelago?
options: Engage in commercial and mining activities
politics.fact: Despite being Norwegian territory, citizens of over 40 nations can legally mine coal on Svalbard.

politics.question: Which international agreement, signed in 2023, established the first-ever legal framework for protecting biodiversity in the high seas?
politics.fact: The BBNJ Treaty covers nearly two-thirds of the ocean that previously had almost no biodiversity protections.

politics.question: Who serves as the President of Indonesia, having taken office in October 2024?
politics.fact: Prabowo lost the presidency twice before winning on his third attempt, after previously being his rival's defense minister.

politics.question: Which sitting world leader holds a PhD in quantum chemistry and previously worked as a research scientist?
politics.fact: Sheinbaum co-authored reports for the IPCC that shared the 2007 Nobel Peace Prize before entering politics.

politics.question: As of March 2026, who is the Prime Minister of Thailand, having risen to power through the Pheu Thai Party?
politics.fact: Paetongtarn became Thailand's youngest-ever PM and the second Shinawatra sibling to hold the office.

politics.question: In 2025, which country's president faced impeachment proceedings partly triggered by declaring martial law that lasted fewer than six hours?
politics.fact: President Yoon Suk-yeol's martial law decree in December 2024 was one of the shortest in modern history.

politics.question: Which European commissioner resigned in 2024 amid a lobbying scandal involving undisclosed meetings with Qatari officials known as 'Qatargate'?
politics.fact: Police found bags stuffed with €600,000 in cash during raids connected to the Qatargate investigation.

politics.question: The 'Watergate of India' nickname was given to which scandal involving alleged stock market manipulation by a major conglomerate?
politics.fact: The Hindenburg report wiped over $100 billion from Adani Group's market value in just two trading sessions.
